current_focus: Track a Pareto frontier across reconstruction quality and sparsity/cost. Treat K=128 as one anchor point, not the only success criterion. Prefer experiments that add non-dominated tradeoff points, including smaller-K runs that accept some FVU increase when they improve the overall frontier.
architecture_findings: r30 gated no_change k? -> policy_reject (不允许 no_change；每一轮都必须提出可执行的实验或修复)

failure_patterns.pattern: After two consecutive K128 loss-path probes (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625` and `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD=100000`) both failed to beat the declared `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` anchor, the most useful single question is whether the exact reference recipe itself still reproduces a clean late-phase descent under the current stack. This round makes no recipe change; it reruns the fully explicit mainline baseline to refresh the curve-shape reference before any further LR or optimizer decisions.

topk.design_hypothesis: Round 26 showed that disabling `COMPILE_MODEL` cleared the `first_step_timeout` and kept the K128 baseline in the same general FVU band, but with a small regression versus the compiled anchor. The next single question is whether the stabilized non-compiled path prefers a slightly different step size; this round changes only `LR`, lowering it from `1.6e-3` to `1.4e-3` while keeping the rest of the stabilized recipe fixed, to test whether the worse curve came from a mildly over-aggressive learning rate on the uncompiled path.
next_steps: If `LR=1.4e-3` improves the stabilized K128 run, do one more narrow LR refinement on the same runtime path before switching optimizer.
If `LR=1.4e-3` is flat or worse, conclude that the stabilized path does not benefit from a lower step size and move to the optimizer axis next.
If the curve shape improves while final FVU stays similar, use that signal to decide whether runtime stability plus smoother training is worth further work even without a frontier gain.

whitened_topk.design_hypothesis: With the `topk` mainline saturated for five rounds, the highest-value matched architecture probe is a directly compatible near-neighbor that changes support formation without changing the exported reconstruction form. `whitened_topk` keeps the same `K=128` sparse weighted-sum decoder contract as `topk` but adds input preconditioning before top-k competition, so this round asks one clean question: does that architectural change alone produce a meaningfully better curve or enough promise to justify follow-up work.
next_steps: If `whitened_topk` is clearly worse than the stabilized `topk` control, archive it immediately and return to mainline topk recipe work next round.
If `whitened_topk` is roughly competitive with a smoother or still-descending curve, record it as a promising probe result but still return to the topk mainline next round per policy.
If `whitened_topk` beats the matched topk control, schedule exactly one later follow-up on the same family after the mandated return to the mainline.

gated.design_hypothesis: Within the `gated` mainline at `K=128, EF=12, optimizer=signum`, a conservative LR step down from `1.6e-3` to `1.2e-3` may improve stability and early-to-mid training curve smoothness enough to beat the current `0.149866` result; this round asks only whether the matched `gated` recipe is slightly overdriven at the reference LR.
next_steps: If `gated` at LR=1.2e-3 is smoother and at least as good, try one upward/downward bracket endpoint next to localize the LR optimum.
If `gated` at LR=1.2e-3 is clearly worse across the curve, keep `1.6e-3` as the gated anchor and consider optimizer swap next.

recent_rounds.hypothesis: Round 19 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=8` suffered a large quality drop and much heavier dead-feature pressure, with the late curve nearly flattening but not fully collapsing. This round changes only `K`, moving from `8` to `4`, to answer the single remaining boundary question: whether the current export-compatible topk recipe has any usable signal left at the absolute minimum allowed K, or whether `K=8` already marks the effective floor.

Round 20 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=4` is effectively past the practical floor: the late-phase FVU curve flattened completely while dead-feature pressure rose to about `0.68`, so further low-K descent is no longer the right question. This round changes only `K`, moving from the quality anchor side to `K=96`, to test whether LUTurbo can obtain a materially cheaper but still high-quality deployment point that may be more practical than the stressed ultra-low-K regime.

Round 21 showed that `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF=12` at `K=96` is a promising high-quality reduced-cost point: its curve kept descending smoothly through the late phase, and both `dead_pct` and `auxk` stayed very low. This round changes only the loss-shaping axis, lowering `AUXK_ALPHA` from `0.03125` to `0.015625` while keeping the `K=96` recipe fixed, to answer whether the new near-anchor deployment point is slightly over-regularized and can recover a bit more reconstruction quality without destabilizing training.
recent_rounds.termination_reason: 当前实际改动主轴是 k，primary_variable 不应写 other_param

recent_rounds.hypothesis: Round 14 showed that lowering `AUXK_ALPHA` below the `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` reference did not improve FVU, which leaves the opposite loss-shaping question open: the current mainline may be slightly under-regularized rather than over-regularized. This round changes only `AUXK_ALPHA`, increasing it from `0.03125` to `0.0625` while keeping the exact K128 reference recipe fixed, to test whether stronger dead-feature pressure improves late-stage reconstruction or curve stability.

Round 23 showed that increasing `AUXK_ALPHA` above the `topk/signum/K128/EF12/LR=1.6e-3` reference did not improve the anchor recipe, which suggests the mainline is likely saturated on auxiliary-loss magnitude itself. This round changes only `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD`, lowering it from the effectively inactive `10000000` to `100000`, to test whether earlier dead-feature rescue improves late-stage reconstruction or curve shape without changing the core topk recipe.

After two consecutive K128 loss-path probes (`AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625` and `DEAD_FEATURE_THRESHOLD=100000`) both failed to beat the declared `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` anchor, the most useful single question is whether the exact reference recipe itself still reproduces a clean late-phase descent under the current stack. This round makes no recipe change; it reruns the fully explicit mainline baseline to refresh the curve-shape reference before any further LR or optimizer decisions.

Round 25 crashed with `first_step_timeout` while rerunning the exact K128 anchor, so the immediate question is not recipe quality but whether the timeout was caused by compile-time startup overhead rather than by the `topk/signum/LR=1.6e-3/K=128/EF=12` recipe itself. This round changes only `COMPILE_MODEL`, turning it off on the exact reference backbone to test whether the baseline can complete cleanly and recover an interpretable curve.

Round 26 showed that disabling `COMPILE_MODEL` cleared the `first_step_timeout` and kept the K128 baseline in the same general FVU band, but with a small regression versus the compiled anchor. The next single question is whether the stabilized non-compiled path prefers a slightly different step size; this round changes only `LR`, lowering it from `1.6e-3` to `1.4e-3` while keeping the rest of the stabilized recipe fixed, to test whether the worse curve came from a mildly over-aggressive learning rate on the uncompiled path.

With the `topk` mainline saturated for five rounds, the highest-value matched architecture probe is a directly compatible near-neighbor that changes support formation without changing the exported reconstruction form. `whitened_topk` keeps the same `K=128` sparse weighted-sum decoder contract as `topk` but adds input preconditioning before top-k competition, so this round asks one clean question: does that architectural change alone produce a meaningfully better curve or enough promise to justify follow-up work.

Round 28 showed that `whitened_topk` is not worth continuing: under a matched probe it failed catastrophically, so the next and only remaining useful question in this probe slot is whether a different directly compatible support-selection variant has any architectural merit at all. `gated` preserves the same static-dictionary weighted-sum reconstruction form as `topk` while changing only how support is selected and scaled, so this round asks a single yes/no question: is `gated` competitive enough under a matched K128/signum/LR=1.6e-3/EF12 probe to justify any future follow-up.

A clean rerun of the matched `gated` baseline at `K=128, EF=12, optimizer=signum, lr=1.6e-3` will tell us whether Round 29 was a stable result with a healthy curve shape or a one-off; if it reproduces near 0.1499 with smooth descent, we should keep `gated` on the mainline and start the LR sweep next.

NOTE: Judge this run by full curve shape as well as final FVU: if earlier rescue smooths late descent or lowers FVU, the issue was activation timing; if not, the current topk/signum/K128 recipe is likely saturated on the aux-loss path.

NOTE: The next clean question is whether that stabilized runtime path wants a slightly smaller LR; treat this as an LR calibration of the non-compiled baseline rather than a direct replacement of the compiled historical anchor.

NOTE: Judge this round by full curve shape as well as final FVU: if `1.4e-3` smooths late descent or recovers the small regression seen at Round 26, keep refining LR on the stabilized path; if not, the non-compiled path likely does not justify further LR tuning before moving to optimizer.

NOTE: Judge this probe by full curve shape plus final FVU relative to the stabilized K128 topk control: if it is clearly worse or unstable, drop the family and return to topk; if it is competitive or better, it earns at most one follow-up slot later, but the immediate next round should still return to the topk mainline.
